problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem A (continuous/dynamics). The task: define the "power curve" (power-vs-duration profile) for a time-trial specialist and a second rider type, accounting for gender; produce an optimal pacing/power-distribution strategy over real time-trial courses; assess sensitivity to weather and to rider deviations from the target power plan; then extend to a six-rider team time trial whose finishing time is set when the 4th rider crosses the line; finally write a 2-page rider guidance memo for a Directeur Sportif. This paper (2209812) treats it as a minimum-time optimal-control problem grounded in exercise physiology and cycling dynamics.
models_used: 3-parameter hyperbolic Critical Power (CP) model (Morton): TTE = W'/(P-CP) + Pmax/(CP-Pmax), chosen over the simpler 2-parameter CP model TTE = W'/(P-CP) on stated physiological grounds (finite Pmax, exhaustion not requiring full W' depletion)

Fatigue-and-Recovery (FR) model with a switching ODE for remaining anaerobic energy w: dw/dt = -(P-CP) when P>=CP, and -(P_adj-CP) when P<CP, with adjusted recovery power P_adj = aP + b (linear in pedaling power) and instantaneous max power Pmax = aw + CP (linear in remaining anaerobic energy)

Newtonian point-mass cycling dynamics: state-space model with states (s distance, v velocity, w anaerobic energy), control u(t)=pedal power; dv/dt from Newton's 2nd law including aerodynamic drag (0.5 Cd rho A v^2), gravity/slope term g(sin theta + C_R cos theta), and rolling resistance; effective mass m = m_b + 2 I_w/R_w^2 accounting for wheel rotational inertia
Minimum-time optimal control formulation: min J = integral dt subject to state-space dynamics and box constraints on v, w in [0,W'], and u in [0, Pmax(w)]

Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP): Hamiltonian H = L + lambda^T f augmented with mu*C and eta^T S^(1) multipliers for the inequality/state constraints; co-state ODEs derived; bang-bang-style result showing optimal power takes values only from {u_max, u for v-dot=0, CP, 0} via a 4-case analysis

Dynamic Programming / Bellman (written 'Behrman') principle of optimality for the numerical solution: backward recursion J*_{i,N} = min_{u_i}[J_{i,i+1} + J*_{i+1,N}], with zero-order-hold discretization of the state equations over distance steps Delta s
Wind model: relative-wind drag F_W = (1/(2m)) Cd rho A [v - V cos(gamma - phi)]^2 using wind angle phi and heading gamma relative to north

Temperature/air-density model: air pressure-altitude relation p = -0.011h + 100.8, air density rho = 1.293 p/101.325 * 273.15/(273.15+t), lapse rate t = t0 - 0.6h/100, so rho = f(t0,h) feeding back into the drag term

Monte-Carlo-style deviation sensitivity: each sample point of the optimal power plan perturbed with probability p; repeated minimum-time evaluations vs deviation probability and vs course section (location)

Team Time Trial optimization model: drag-reduction factor C_dr for drafting (D = 0.5 rho C_dr Cd A v^2) plus a rotating pace-line lead-time optimization min J = f(Delta_1..Delta_8) over each rider's time-in-front, with team time set by the 4th finisher
key_techniques: Anchoring the model in established exercise-physiology literature (Morton 3-param CP; Ashtiani et al. fatigue/recovery; Keller's optimal-velocity-in-a-race) rather than inventing ad hoc curves, lending physiological credibility

Casting pacing as a constrained optimal-control problem and deriving necessary conditions analytically via PMP before solving numerically — analytic structure (the 4-case bang-bang result reducing optimal u to a discrete set) plus DP numerics
Reformulating the cost with distance s (not time t) as the independent variable so the course discretizes naturally into Delta s nodes for backward DP

Using real GPS course data: STRAVA tracks for the 2021 Tokyo Olympic and UCI Flanders time trials, plus a self-designed San Felice Circeo course; converting lat/long/altitude into segment distances (great-circle L=R*c) and slopes for the dynamics
Calibrating power curves to TrainingPeaks power-profile benchmark data at 5s/1min/5min/FT for four rider archetypes (male/female x time-trialist/sprinter)

Multi-axis sensitivity analysis: wind (speed x direction 3D surfaces per course), temperature (via density feedback), deviation probability, and deviation location, each tied back to an actionable conclusion
Coupling the weather sub-models back into the same core dynamics (wind into drag, temperature into rho) so sensitivity reuses one engine rather than separate models
Translating quantitative results into a concrete coach-facing memo with three pacing principles (go hard at start, hold at CP, rest downhill / push uphill)
why_it_won: "It reads like a graduate-level optimal-control study rather than a heuristic essay. The judges reward a model that is physically principled (Newtonian dynamics + physiology), mathematically rigorous (PMP necessary conditions with co-state equations and a clean bang-bang characterization), and computationally honest (backward DP on a distance grid). Critically, it validated on real, named courses (Tokyo, UCI Flanders) with reproducible STRAVA data and produced plausible finishing times (e.g. men's TT specialist ~3641 s). The sensitivity work is unusually thorough — four distinct sensitivity dimensions, each yielding a coach-actionable takeaway — which directly answers the problem's 'how sensitive' questions. The model is also coherent end-to-end: the same drag/dynamics core is reused for wind, temperature, deviation, and team-drafting, showing one unified framework rather than disconnected pieces. Finally the rider-guidance memo cleanly converts math into decisions, which the problem explicitly demanded."
section_highlights: "Strong structure: clear Task-grouped sections (Tasks 1&2, 3&4, 5) each mapped in an explicit 'Our Work' flowchart; literature-review framework diagram positioning their model choices; a notations table and explicit, individually-justified assumptions (brakes negligible, lateral motion <1% of speed, uniform bikes for 'fair play', constant aero posture). The model build escalates logically: 2-param CP -> its 4 physiological flaws -> 3-param CP -> FR switching dynamics -> full state-space -> optimal control -> PMP -> DP numerics. Results are presented as power-vs-distance overlaid on elevation profiles for every course and a consolidated minimum-time table (Table 3). Sensitivity uses 3D wind surfaces, a temperature deviation curve, and a radar plot for per-section location sensitivity (Table 4 with arrival-time ± deviations at critical km points). Explicit Strengths/Weaknesses section and a polished 2-page Directeur Sportif memo with the four guidance questions answered."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy reliance on a single source (Ashtiani et al. arXiv 2007.11393) for the optimal-control formulation and the a,b / Pmax=aw+CP relations — the modeling contribution is largely a re-implementation rather than novel, which a sharp judge could discount

Visible sloppiness/typos that undercut polish: 'Behrman' for Bellman throughout, 'Pursuiter'/'Rouleur' listed as rider types but only TT-specialist vs sprinter actually used, an undefined 'Equation (ddd)' reference, and inconsistent notation (mu vs C_R for rolling resistance between Eqs 13 and 31)

Data-quality issues in the rendered figures: several result charts (e.g. Figure 10/11/17 tables) contain implausible or garbled values (power jumping to 1600 mid-course, deviation table trailing into '...'), so the simulation outputs are hard to verify and some results look physically inconsistent
Constraint v <= v_max assumed constant even through sharp turns — explicitly acknowledged but a real weakness for cornering-heavy courses
Air density assumed constant in the core dynamics (Eq 12) yet treated as temperature/altitude-dependent in the sensitivity section — an internal inconsistency

Team time trial model (Task 5) is the weakest: the objective J = f(Delta_1..Delta_8) and the lead-time terms are stated abstractly with no actual solved optimum, no numerical team result, and the drag-reduction coefficient C_dr never quantified — Task 5 is essentially under-delivered
Self-acknowledged omission of air vortices/turbulence and atmospheric-pressure drag effects at >10 m/s speeds
reusable_patterns: For any min-time / optimal-pacing problem: model as constrained optimal control (state-space + box constraints), derive PMP necessary conditions to show the optimal control is bang-bang over a small discrete set, then solve numerically with backward DP — a template transferable to energy-budget, robot-path, or resource-depletion problems
Switch the independent variable from time to distance (or another monotone state) so a physical course/path discretizes cleanly into DP nodes

Ground 'profile/curve' definition tasks in published benchmark data (here TrainingPeaks power profiles) and fit an established physiological/empirical model instead of inventing one — fast credibility
Use real geospatial data (STRAVA/GPS lat-long-altitude) and a documented pipeline (great-circle distance + slope extraction) to make a model concrete and validatable on named real-world instances

Build one core physics engine (drag + slope + rolling resistance dynamics) and reuse it for every sub-question, injecting weather as parameter perturbations (wind into relative-velocity drag, temperature into air density) rather than building separate models
Structure sensitivity analysis along multiple independent axes (environmental: wind, temperature; behavioral: deviation magnitude, deviation location) and end each with a one-sentence actionable rule

Open each task section with a 'Our Work' flowchart and a literature-framework diagram to orient judges, and close with an explicit Strengths/Weaknesses honest self-assessment plus a decision-maker memo that converts numbers into a short ranked list of recommendations
